title: Iran Is Hitting Saudi Energy Infrastructure Hard || Peter Zeihan

transcript.text: Good morning all. Peter Zion here coming
to you from Colorado. Today is the 7th
of April and overnight the Iranians have
punched through this Saudi missile
defense system for the first time in a
significant way. Uh seven ballistic
missiles targeted the industrial city of
Jabal which is on the west side of the
Gulf about halfway roughly between
Kuwait and Gutter. Uh, according to
Saudi reports, the seven missiles were
all intercepted, but the debris, which
is Russian for we missed one. Uh,
got through and did significant damage
to a petrochemical complex. Uh, this is
important for two reasons. Number one,
most Saudi assets are shielded to a
degree from directian attack by gutter
and the UAE. Uh, and so those two
countries have depleted most of their
defensive munitions and are now
basically open to attack. Uh, it's a
little bit more complicated that, but
that's kind of the the core idea. If the
Iranians really want to hit something in
those two countries or Kuwait, they
really can. Uh, but Saudi Arabia, most
of its assets being behind that to the
west of them hasn't had to worry about
that as much. Well, now they do. Uh,
Jabal is one of the largest
petrochemical complexes in the world.
It's right next to Rosenura, which is
the world's largest oil loading facility
for super tankers. And it's right next
to Obcock, which is the world's largest
oil processing facility, which allows
the crude to go from the fields and into
the pipelines. Obcock is also the
primary feedin for the pro line, which
allows Saudi exports to partially bypass
the straight of Hormuz completely and go
west to Yanbu. All of them are in the
same general arc. And if the Iranians
have now had success in Jabal, they'll
probably have success in the rest of
those. Uh that means the single largest
concentration of crude on the planet
that goes to Robcock uh is now in direct
danger and we will probably be seeing
more and more damage to that. Uh that
means even though the Saudis have a
bypass pipeline doesn't really matter
anymore because they're not going to be
able to put crude into it. So in the
short term we're talking about a million
barrels per day of refining capacity
offline. We'll probably have another
million barrels per day equivalent to
prochemical capacity going offline in
the next few days. And if OB gets hit in
any meaningful way, that's another 5 to
7 million barrels a day of crude that's
will vanish from the market for years.
